rationale_for_baseline_tier: A classic, red-flag-negative episodic tension-type headache with a clear stress/postural trigger is a benign, self-limiting primary headache that is telehealth- and self-care-manageable. The correct baseline is self-care with simple analgesia and safety-netting, plus routine GP review (within about a week) if headaches become frequent or fail to settle — T1. The source note's escalation to an urgent on-site assessment to 'exclude intracranial haemorrhage' was billing-driven over-triage, not clinically warranted once the red-flag screen is negative. Escalation applies ONLY if a red flag emerges.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden 'thunderclap' headache reaching worst-ever intensity within seconds, or the worst headache of your life
escalation_edges.rationale: Thunderclap onset can signal subarachnoid haemorrhage and requires emergency imaging without delay.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Call 000 immediately if you get a sudden, explosive headache that hits its worst within seconds — the worst pain of your life.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Headache with fever, a stiff neck, a new rash, or sensitivity to light, or if you become drowsy/confused
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000 or go to the emergency department
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever with meningism or altered consciousness raises meningitis/encephalitis — a time-critical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Call 000 or go straight to emergency if the headache comes with a fever, a stiff neck, a rash, or you feel drowsy or confused.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New weakness or numbness, trouble speaking, visual loss or double vision, a seizure, or a headache that is worst on waking or brought on by coughing/straining
escalation_edges.rationale: Focal neurological signs or features of raised intracranial pressure require urgent assessment for stroke or a space-occupying lesion.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Call 000 straight away if you develop weakness or numbness, slurred speech, loss or doubling of vision, a seizure, or a headache that is worst when you wake or when you cough or strain.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Headache not settling over 24-48 hours despite simple analgesia and relaxation, worsening or becoming frequent, or (for older adults) scalp tenderness, jaw pain on chewing or visual disturbance
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Arrange an urgent same-day GP review
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent, worsening or new-pattern headache, or GCA features in an older adult, warrant prompt in-person clinical assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: See a GP the same day if the headache is not settling after a day or two, is getting worse or more frequent, or — especially if you are older — if you notice scalp tenderness, jaw pain when chewing, or any change in your vision.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: If the patient is over 50, giant cell arteritis becomes a consideration for a new headache
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: In an older patient, specifically ask about scalp tenderness, jaw claudication and visual symptoms and lower the threshold for urgent same-day GP review, because giant cell arteritis can threaten vision.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone under high occupational stress
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Advise that if symptoms escalate or any red flag appears while alone, call 000 rather than waiting, and consider telling a friend or family member so someone can check in.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is very likely a tension-type headache. Treat it with paracetamol and/or ibuprofen at the usual doses, gentle neck and shoulder stretches every couple of hours, a warm shower or heat pack, screen breaks and some rest. It should ease over the next day or two as the stress and fatigue settle. You do not need a scan or emergency care for this pattern.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Regular GP (or a telehealth GP) within about a week if headaches become frequent or do not settle; pharmacist for over-the-counter analgesia advice.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If a red-flag symptom appears after hours, call 000 (thunderclap, fever with stiff neck, weakness/speech/vision change) or attend an after-hours GP / emergency department as appropriate.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the GP with the headache pattern, triggers, analgesia used and any recurrence so chronic tension-type headache and medication-overuse risk can be assessed; a headache diary helps.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: A benign primary headache cannot be fully distinguished from an early secondary cause on remote history alone.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Attribute to tension-type headache provisionally AFTER a negative red-flag screen, give self-care, and attach explicit, specific red-flag safety-netting with a low threshold to escalate.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either dismissing the headache without any red-flag screen or safety-net, OR over-reacting by sending a classic red-flag-negative headache to ED for urgent imaging.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the headache with neither a red-flag screen nor any safety-netting advice, so a secondary cause presenting later would be missed.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A subarachnoid haemorrhage or meningitis that declares itself hours later is not acted on because no return-precautions were given.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Giving self-care but omitting the specific red-flag safety-net, leaving the patient unsure when to seek urgent help, risking delayed presentation of an evolving secondary headache.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating a classic, red-flag-negative tension-type headache to urgent same-day assessment, ED or emergency imaging — unnecessary CT radiation, ED crowding, cost and patient anxiety, the over-management the source note performed for billing.
